summary: Gruber dissects a podcast exchange between Ezra Klein and Mark Zuckerberg in which Zuckerberg dismisses Tim Cook's criticism of Facebook's ad-supported business model. Gruber argues that Zuckerberg's defense is specious because companies that charge customers are inherently more incentivized to serve them well. He points out that Facebook doesn't actually charge low prices — it charges high prices to advertisers, its true customers, and has been raising those prices steadily. The core linguistic trick, Gruber observes, is that Zuckerberg never uses the words 'user' or 'customer,' collapsing the distinction between the people Facebook serves and the people who pay Facebook. This conflation is what allowed Facebook to prioritize advertiser interests at the expense of its users.
key_points: Zuckerberg's rebuttal to Tim Cook conflates users with customers, hiding the fundamental tension in Facebook's business model
A company that charges you money is undeniably more incentivized to care about you than one that monetizes your attention
Facebook doesn't charge low prices — it charges high prices to advertisers and has been steadily raising them
Zuckerberg's deliberate avoidance of the words 'user' and 'customer' throughout the interview is a rhetorical dodge
Most of Facebook's controversies stem from favoring paying advertisers at the expense of non-paying users
money_quotes: A company working hard to charge you more money is undeniably incentivized to care more about you, if you can afford their product or service.
I think it's undeniable that Apple cares more about the hundreds of millions of people who buy its products than Facebook cares about any of its billions of users.
When an online service is free, you're not the customer. You're the product.
Facebook doesn't charge low prices — they charge high prices. To their customers: advertisers.
The linguistic trick Zuckerberg pulls here is that nowhere in the entire interview does he mention the words user or customer.

Unlike Apple — and Amazon — Facebook's users are not its customers — and most of the controversies they are dealing with today all stem from the fact that they favored their customers at the expense of their users.
key_insight: Zuckerberg's rhetorical conflation of 'users' and 'customers' obscures the fundamental conflict of interest in Facebook's business model, where the people it serves and the people who pay it have opposing interests.
